fund.name: Motilal Oswal Large and Midcap Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Good mix of large and mid-sized companies with strong long-term returns. This fund has done well over 5 years and charges a low yearly fee. It fits your 8-15 year goals and moderate risk comfort.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Low-cost, simple index fund tracking midcap stocks. Lower yearly fee than active funds, which helps in the long run. Adds smart diversification beyond large and midcap stocks.

fund.name: quant Multi Asset Allocation Fund-GROWTH OPTION-Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: A simple mix of stocks, bonds, and other assets in one fund. It has been stable and steady. Since you're new to investing, this one fund gives you safety while staying tax-smart. Used for lumpsum from existing corpus to balance risk.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Groww or Kuvera. Step 2: Upload your ID and bank details to complete KYC. Step 3: Invest ₹50,000 as a lumpsum in the quant Multi Asset Allocation Fund. Step 4: Start monthly SIPs: ₹10,800 in Motilal Oswal Large and Midcap Fund and ₹2,700 in Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund. Step 5: Set a monthly reminder on your phone for SIP day.
review_checkpoints: After 3 months: confirm all SIPs are running and money is being deducted.
After 6 months: check if any fund is underperforming compared to similar funds.
After 1 year: review annual returns and your comfort level with ups and downs.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, check how much each fund has grown. If one fund's value has gone up a lot and now makes more than 10% of your total money than planned, shift some of it to the others to get back to your original plan.
plan.rationale: At 29, you have 25+ years to build wealth, so we've kept 75% in stocks that grow faster. ₹1.8 lakh already saved is a strong start. We've used ₹50,000 to add a safer option that also grows over time. The rest is invested monthly in proven funds that mix big and growing companies. Since you earn modestly and invest every month, low-cost and simple funds were picked so you can understand and trust each one. You're not chasing FIRE, but freedom — this mix aims to grow steadily with moderate risk, just like your journey.
risks: Stock markets can drop 30% in a bad year — your fund value may fall temporarily. This is normal.
Midcap and small cap funds can be more bumpy than large caps. We've kept them balanced to avoid big swings.
No fund is risk-free. Past returns don't guarantee future results, but disciplined investing works over time.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth

aps.reasoning: 1) passive_instrument_fraction: only the 15% Mid-cap 150 Index fund is passive equity; the 60% Large-&-Mid-cap fund and the equity sleeve inside the 25% hybrid fund are active. 15% / 75% ≈ 0.2. 2) turnover_score: annual rebalancing threshold (±10% drift) and low-touch 6-month/1-year checks give a largely buy-and-hold stance; score 0.8. 3) cost_emphasis_score: expense ratios are mentioned and the index fund is chosen for low cost, but the main active equity fund (1.1%) and hybrid fund (0.85%) are accepted without heavy cost focus; score 0.4. 4) research_vs_cost_score: rationale stresses past performance and fund track records rather than deep security analysis, but still justifies each fund on merit; moderately passive on research depth, score 0.6. 5) time_horizon_alignment_score: 25-year view, no tactical calls, only annual re-balance; score 0.9. 6) portfolio_activeness_score: the equity portion has weighted beta ≈1.07 and very high tracking error (≈12%) plus large positive alphas, indicating genuine active bets; only 15% is index-like. Blended equity activeness therefore low on the passive scale, score 0.25.

pqs.reasoning:
goal_alignment: The plan targets moderate-risk, long-term (15-year) freedom rather than FIRE, which fits, but the portfolio is tilted to 75% equity with a strong mid-cap bias and only a hybrid fund for debt, slightly aggressive for a moderate-risk investor who also wants a safety cushion in 8 years for her creative venture.

diversification: Domestic equity only; no large-cap-only core, no small-cap, international, debt-fund or gold sleeve; 60% in one active fund, another 15% in single mid-cap index, all from the same AMC—concentration risk on style, sector (mid-caps) and fund house.

risk_return_appropriateness: 14.3% CAGR projection is ambitious yet within the MF market range; risk warnings mention big falls, but debt sleeve is a conservative-hybrid lumpsum plus a 5-yr extraordinary outlier past-return rationale instead of pure low-duration debt for a 6-month emergency mention. Allocation is thus a bit aggressive for “moderate”, especially with the mid-cap skew.

internal_consistency: Numbers add up, equity/debt split matches funds, reviews and rebalancing are clear, only minor nit that 75/25 equity/debt diagram mis-classifies conservative hybrid (≈25% equity) as 100% debt but no material contradiction; overall coherent narrative.

tax_efficiency: Chooses equity-taxed vehicles for the main SIPs (good for a new-regime 30% effective taxpayer), uses conservative-hybrid (non-equity) only for the lumpsum debt role and doesn’t clutter portfolio with ELSS; no obvious tax-blunder, though it omits arbitrage or equity-savings for a pure debt sleeve if desired.
